caveats: a: The occupation source is NAICS 812200, which includes funeral homes as well as 812220.
a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and provides occupation employment, not task-level wage exposure.
a: Cemetery-heavy and crematory-heavy firms have materially different work mixes.
rho: There is no industry-specific measured five-year AI implementation rate.
rho: Implementation will vary sharply with record digitization, site count, and state compliance burden.
e: The frozen n=39 is an ESTIMATE derived through an industry-margin bridge.
e: Establishment classifications do not reveal control rights, nonprofit restrictions, or whether perpetual-care assets are separable.
e: Pet cemeteries may differ from human death-care facilities in scale and regulation.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry, intention-based, and not specific to the LMM band.
s5: Internal family succession, asset sales without operations, and nonprofit reorganizations are not qualifying transfers.

q: No source directly measures pass-through of automation savings in this industry.
d5: Deaths are an event-count proxy, not constant-quality service quantity.
d5: Cremation projections are from an industry association and cover all disposition channels, not only 812220.
d5: The combined NAICS contains cemetery and crematory businesses that benefit differently from the same mix shift.

o: Vertical integration into funeral homes can move activity outside 812220 without eliminating physical disposition work.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are families, estate representatives, funeral homes, and pet owners. Revenue is primarily transaction- and contract-based through interment or cremation charges, cemetery property and memorial rights, and pre-need arrangements, with some recurring care or maintenance fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Funeral homes combined with crematories, which Census places outside 812220; public or religious captive units, shells, non-control financing and perpetual-care vehicles, and operations without a transferable external-customer service business.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators of cemeteries, memorial gardens, mausoleums, pet cemeteries, and stand-alone crematories that provide repeat or recurring disposition, interment, plot, memorial-property, records, and care services to external families or funeral-service customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader four-digit Death Care Services industry, not 812220, and reports employment rather than wage-weighted task exposure. I map most routine office, records, scheduling, bookkeeping, customer-message, and marketing work, plus smaller fractions of management and arranging work, to AI-exposed tasks; I map cremation, interment, grounds, maintenance, and emotionally sensitive in-person work mostly to non-exposed tasks. Because funeral homes likely carry more arranging and office labor than stand-alone cemeteries and crematories, the broader-industry mix may overstate exposure for 812220, while using employment rather than wage weights may understate higher-paid administrative and management exposure.
caveats: The occupation source is NAICS 812200, which includes funeral homes as well as 812220.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers and provides occupation employment, not task-level wage exposure.
Cemetery-heavy and crematory-heavy firms have materially different work mixes.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from a representative sample of stand-alone cemetery and crematory operators, separating already-automated work from remaining tasks, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: In May 2023, office and administrative support was 19.41% of Death Care Services employment, management 12.78%, sales 2.68%, grounds/building work 11.76%, and personal care and service 46.01%; crematory operators alone were 2.03%. The exposed share is therefore meaningful but bounded by substantial physical and accountable service work. [S2]

d5.bridge: CDC deaths proxy the event base for human disposition demand, while NFDA's national cremation-versus-burial shares proxy mix within the current service basket. I hold price and quality constant, allow a modest increase as aging supports event volumes, and widen the range because final U.S. deaths fell from 3,090,964 in 2023 to 3,072,666 in 2024. Rising cremation supports stand-alone crematories but can reduce cemetery interment intensity, so the combined 812220 effect is smaller than the cremation-share shift. The proxy omits pet-death demand and could understate pet-cemetery growth.
caveats: Deaths are an event-count proxy, not constant-quality service quantity.
Cremation projections are from an industry association and cover all disposition channels, not only 812220.
The combined NAICS contains cemetery and crematory businesses that benefit differently from the same mix shift.
d5.change_evidence: Census service-line revenue quantities or trade-association case counts for stand-alone cremations, cemetery interments, plot rights, and pet dispositions through 2031 would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Final resident deaths were broadly stable but down 0.6% in 2024, while NFDA projected cremation at 63.4% and burial at 31.6% in 2025. The sector's essential event base is durable, but mix shifts redistribute demand between cemetery and crematory services. [S4] [S5]

caveats: The frozen n=39 is an ESTIMATE derived through an industry-margin bridge.
Establishment classifications do not reveal control rights, nonprofit restrictions, or whether perpetual-care assets are separable.
Pet cemeteries may differ from human death-care facilities in scale and regulation.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 812220 ownership form, tax status, related funeral-home ownership, EBITDA, and willingness to sell control would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is coherent around disposition facilities, but the LMM firm population includes cemetery associations, memorial gardens, pet cemeteries, mausoleums, and stand-alone crematories. Public, religious, nonprofit, perpetual-care, family-trust, and funeral-home-linked structures can fail the transferable-control lens even when their establishments appear in the industry. The frozen firm-count input is also margin-bridged rather than observed.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 812220 ($1,703,099,633) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 812220 ($5,745,881,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[81]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $176,022 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 812220); class <5: 2,368 firms, avg revenue/firm $199,087 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,157 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 695 firms, avg revenue/firm $820,784 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $128,453 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 318 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,896,494 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $296,801 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 217 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,163,912 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $808,152 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 39 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,207,487 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,005,972 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $159,375,071 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,942,199 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 39

caveats: The estimate concerns accountable operators, not the amount of human labor inside each case.
Vertical integration into funeral homes can move activity outside 812220 without eliminating physical disposition work.
o.change_evidence: Disposition-chain studies measuring the share of cases completed through direct self-service, municipal alternatives, or vertically integrated funeral homes without an independent 812220 operator would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Nearly all year-five quantity still requires an accountable operator to maintain a site or crematory, verify authority and identity, preserve records, handle remains, operate equipment, and complete interment or cremation. Online arrangements and direct-disposition choices can remove some front-office touchpoints but do not eliminate the physical operator.

caveats: Billing practices and pre-need regulation vary by state and operator type.
No source directly measures pass-through of automation savings in this industry.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples and transaction-level price, discount, renewal, referral-channel, and labor-cost data before and after automation from multi-site operators would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Retail prices are usually set per property right or service rather than by disclosed labor hours, allowing some implemented benefit to remain with the operator. Retention is reduced by local price comparison, funeral-home referral bargaining, pre-need contracts or trust-funded obligations that delay repricing, and pressure to reinvest savings in service quality, grounds, compliance, and capacity.

caveats: There is no industry-specific measured five-year AI implementation rate.
Implementation will vary sharply with record digitization, site count, and state compliance burden.
rho.change_evidence: Operator-level deployment data from cemetery-management software vendors, including automation feature activation, exception rates, and labor hours before and after implementation, would materially change the range.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be strongest in inquiry handling, document preparation, scheduling, records search, billing support, and marketing. Adoption is constrained by fragmented legacy systems, state-specific authorization and records requirements, error intolerance, emotionally sensitive communications, and the need for human review at the point of disposition.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans among U.S. employer-business owners across industries: 22% planned to sell or transfer ownership. I reduce that rate for plan non-completion, family transfers that do not create an acquirable control transaction, nonprofit or trust restrictions, and cemetery-specific transaction complexity. The broad survey could overstate qualifying transfers because intentions are not completed deals, but it could understate this older, asset-backed sector if ownership is older than the employer-business average.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry, intention-based, and not specific to the LMM band.
Internal family succession, asset sales without operations, and nonprofit reorganizations are not qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed 812220 control transactions linked to the eligible-firm denominator, plus owner-age and succession-plan data from cemetery and crematory trade associations, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A qualifying control transfer is materially narrower than an owner's stated plan to sell or transfer. Gallup nonetheless supports a nontrivial five-year succession pool, while the industry's land, licenses, care obligations, and ownership structures justify a substantial haircut. [S3]

principal_driver: Essential, local physical disposition demand makes most of the service basket durable and still operator-required.
principal_weakness: Transferability is uncertain because ownership form, perpetual-care obligations, and funeral-home affiliation are invisible in the available firm counts.
